content.problemStatement:
You are given a binary tree and three nodes A, B and C. Find the lowest common ancestor of the three nodes.




A binary tree is a tree data structure in which every node has at most 2 children nodes.
Lowest common ancestor of node A, B and C  is defined as the lowest node in the tree such that subtree rooted at that node contains all the nodes A,B and C.
A subtree of a tree T is a tree S consisting of a node in T and all of its descendants in T.

Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
First line of each testcase contains single integer NNN - number of nodes in Tree
Next n lines each contains 2 space separated integers parentiparent_iparenti​ and nodeinode_inodei​ denoting value of parent and node in the given tree.

Note that node with value 1 is the root node and its parent will be given as -1


Last line of each testcase contains nodeanode_anodea​, nodebnode_bnodeb​ and nodecnode_cnodec​ denoting node values for Node A, B and C.

Following things are guaranteed :



nodeanode_anodea​, nodebnode_bnodeb​ and nodecnode_cnodec​ are distinct and will be present in the tree.
Resulting tree is a binary tree.

**Note:**



For C++ language, you need to:

Complete the function in the submit solution tab:


`int  tripletLCA(Node* root,  int a, int b, int c){...}

`
 \  



Output Format
Using the functions you have completed, values returned will be checked for the correctness.



For each testcase a single integer LCALCALCA - least common ancestor will be outputted.
constraints: 4 ≤ N ≤ 100000 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 100000)
The given edges always form a binary tree (each node has at most two children).
a, b, c are distinct and exist in the tree.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the tree.
- The next N lines each contain two space‑separated integers p and v, where p is the parent of node v. The root node has value 1 and its parent is given as -1.
- The last line of the test case contains three distinct integers a, b and c – the values of the three query nodes.
All node values are between 1 and N inclusive.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the value of the lowest common ancestor of nodes a, b and c.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Testcase 1: The tree is built as shown in the statement picture. Nodes 6, 4 and 5 are under node 2, so LCA is 2.\nTestcase 2: Nodes 2, 3 and 4 are all in the left subtree of node 2, therefore the lowest common ancestor is node 2.
Root is 1. Nodes 2 and 3 are its direct children, node 4 is a child of 2. The LCA of 2,3,4 is the root 1.
The tree is a single chain 1-2-3-4-5. Node 2 is ancestor of both 3 and 4, so the LCA of 2,3,4 is 2.

solution.approach:
For each test case we build a parent array parent[v] and compute the depth of every node by a simple BFS/DFS starting from the root (node 1). The LCA of two nodes can be found in O(depth) by:
1. Lift the deeper node until both nodes are on the same depth.
2. Move both nodes up together until they meet – that node is the LCA.
To obtain the LCA of three nodes we first compute l = LCA(a, b) and then answer = LCA(l, c). The whole procedure is linear in the size of the tree.
The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(N) additional memory for parent and depth arrays.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (building the tree + two LCA queries).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for parent and depth arrays.
